questions.question: Describe the 2‑approximation algorithm for Minimum Vertex Cover based on a maximal matching and prove its approximation ratio.
keyPoints: Compute a maximal matching in the graph.
Select both endpoints of each matched edge as the vertex cover.
Any vertex cover must contain at least one endpoint of each matching edge, so |C| ≤ 2·OPT.
The algorithm runs in polynomial time.

questions.question: Explain the notion of hardness of approximation. State the PCP theorem and give an example of a problem (such as Max‑3SAT or Set Cover) that cannot be approximated within a constant factor unless P = NP.
keyPoints: Definition of ρ‑approximation and the implication that no ρ‑approx exists under P≠NP.
PCP theorem: NP = PCP[O(log n), O(1)].
Max‑3SAT is NP‑hard to approximate within factor 7/8+ε.
Set Cover is NP‑hard to approximate within factor (1‑ε)·ln n.

questions.question: Design a fully polynomial‑time approximation scheme (FPTAS) for the 0/1 Knapsack problem. Describe the scaling and dynamic‑programming steps, analyse the running time, and prove that for any ε>0 the algorithm returns a solution of value at least (1‑ε)·OPT.
keyPoints: Sort items by value, consider scaling values by ⌊v_i / ε·OPT⌋.
Run DP on scaled values: DP[w] = max scaled value for weight w.
Show that scaled solution is at least (1‑ε)·OPT using bound on rounding error.

questions.question: Consider the metric Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP). Describe the double‑spanning‑tree algorithm that yields a 2‑approximation. Explain how to transform the spanning tree into a Hamiltonian tour via shortcutting and prove the approximation ratio. Discuss known hardness results and improvements (e.g., Christofides algorithm achieving a 1.5‑approximation).
keyPoints: Build a minimum spanning tree (MST) on the metric distance matrix.
Duplicate all edges of the MST to obtain an Eulerian multigraph.
Find an Eulerian tour and shortcut repeated vertices using triangle inequality to get a TSP tour of length ≤ 2·OPT.
Metric TSP is APX‑hard; Christofides uses a matching on odd‑degree vertices to improve bound to 1.5·OPT.
